reason: Both correctly convey high alarm, treat breakage as routine, propose remote config/kill-switch, telemetry, synthetic monitoring, resilient selectors, and acquisition diversification. The decisive difference is that Answer B explicitly includes converting extension users into direct account/email relationships ('Convert extension users into direct users fast: get accounts/emails early so a broken extension doesn't mean losing the relationship'), one of the four key points, while Answer A never mentions capturing users into a direct relationship at all — a meaningful omission given it's called out as essential. B is also organized with a useful urgency-based timeline (this week/month/quarter) without adding padding, and correctly reframes the risk as fragility rather than overselling policy/ToS risk as A somewhat overweights. Both are otherwise comparable in technical accuracy and conciseness, but B's coverage is more complete on the stated key points.
